cases.title: Subway Blast Survivor
cases.presentation: A bomb detonates in a subway car. A 30-year-old survivor walks out with ringing ears and mild chest tightness. Vitals are initially normal. Ninety minutes later in the treatment area he is dyspneic, tachypneic, and coughing blood-streaked sputum.
cases.question: What is happening and what caution applies?
options: B: Delayed blast lung — give oxygen, escalate care, and if ventilation is needed use the lowest effective pressures, watching for pneumothorax and air embolism.
D: He was fine initially, so no blast injury is possible.
cases.explanation: Enclosed-space blasts multiply primary injury, and blast lung classically declares itself hours later with dyspnea, hypoxia, and hemoptysis. Oxygenate and escalate, but ventilate gently — positive pressure can extend alveolar rupture into pneumothorax or arterial air embolism.
cases.clinicalPearl: The blast survivor who 'walked away' hasn't finished declaring himself — enclosed-space exposures earn observation.

cases.title: The Second Package
cases.presentation: A package bomb detonates outside a courthouse, downing several victims. As the first EMS crew arrives, a police officer points out an unattended second backpack 20 meters from the casualties.
cases.question: What is the correct EMS action?
options: A: Rush to the casualties — seconds count.
B: Stage at a safe distance until law enforcement addresses the suspected device; operate under unified command; enter only when directed.
C: Move the backpack away from the casualties.
D: Have bystanders carry the victims to you through the uncleared zone.
cases.explanation: Secondary devices are placed to kill responders. EMS stages until the device threat is addressed, integrates with law enforcement under unified command, and enters per the coordinated plan. A blown-up medic rescues no one.
cases.clinicalPearl: At intentional scenes, the first casualties you protect are your own crew — stage, coordinate, then treat.

cases.title: First Wave at Memorial ED
cases.presentation: Eight minutes after a marketplace bombing, 30 self-transported patients with lacerations and TM complaints crowd Memorial's ED. The charge physician wants to assign every open bed. You are the hospital incident commander.
cases.question: What is the correct capacity decision?
options: A: Fill all beds now with the walking wounded.
B: Cohort the minor casualties in a fast-track area and hold resuscitation bays and OR capacity for the critical wave arriving by EMS in 30-60 minutes.
C: Close the ED to further arrivals.
D: Send the walking wounded home without assessment.
cases.explanation: Bombing arrivals run upside-down: the self-evacuated minor casualties arrive first, and the critical patients — still being extricated — arrive later. Command protects resuscitation capacity for that second wave while safely cohorting and assessing the first.
cases.clinicalPearl: After a bombing, your first 30 patients are rarely your sickest 30 — plan beds for the wave you haven't met yet.
